comments.text: There’s a chance you’re seeing a lot of green today, like the 2022 Lake Erie harmful algal bloom pictured here. NOAA uses advanced tech to detect toxins found in blooms like this. Find out more ways NOAA is working for you: noaa.gov/news/5-ways-noaa-research-is-making-your-life-better-right-now
This plant was a surprise at a beach on Lake Erie between Cleveland and Buffalo. It's winged pigweed and turns into a tumbleweed when it dries out. There were quite a few of them.

Maybe you don’t care about Lake Erie or Ohio, fine, I get it, but .bsky.social did publish a book that goes deep on the science of algae just a few months ago and WHAT OTHER PUBLISHER worked on an algae cover, I ask you!

Humans of the watershed. You know what this STINKY green mat is- cladophora. It is native to #GreatLakes but is impacting freshwater ecosystems, beaches and water activities. Why the overgrowth? 🌊🌊
Phosphorus runoff (lawns, farms,stormwater, etc)
Quagga mussels Climate change

As Lake Superior warms, it's growing greenish clouds of blue-green algae, which is actually a bacteria. One researcher is tracking it himself, saying authorities aren't acting fast enough to educate the public about preventing blooms and avoiding their dangers. thenarwhal.ca/lake-superio...

Ohio created 10 wetlands to protect Lake Erie - and it worked! But the government couldn't allow that. #nature #wetlands #publicland #wildlife #science #conservation #ProtectNature #NoPlanetB #Politics #News #Activism #EnvironmentalActivism

Officials with Parks Canada are reporting a blue-green algae bloom in Pukaskwa National Park on Lake Superior (again — after one in August). Here's our story about these blooms — or cyanobacteria — and why they're showing up in the north now, too: thenarwhal.ca/lake-superio...

The Raisin River watershed covers part of southeast Michigan. It's filled with sediment and fertilizer from surface runoff because most farmers won't create riparian buffers. The river empties into Lake Erie, where eutrophication causes toxic algal blooms in summer. Blissfield Michigan, 24 Jun 2025.
Sunlight, algae and ice: How 3 Great Lakes scientists won an international award #Canada Research focuses on underwater organisms in Lake Erie www.cbc.ca/news/canada/...

Once again, federal investments are paying off for NW Ohio. BGSU’s Shallow Run Watershed Project is showing progress to improve Lake Erie water quality. Led by Dr. Jay Martin, this partnership of farmers, researchers, and local leaders proves new practices can cut runoff and protect our Great Lakes!

Today, NOAA and Ohio Sea Grant will announce the 2025 Harmful Algal Bloom (HAB) forecast for Lake Erie. This forecast indicates the severity of the bloom season, enabling coastal management and drinking water facility management to take preventive action.

Wanna see something wild? 🧪 On March 15, 2020, the ESA's Copernicus Sentinel-3 captured a series of images of The Great Lakes from orbit (stitched together here) that show the extent of algal blooms, high turbidity, and other water quality issues, ESPECIALLY for Lake Erie. 😬
The blooms have become an annual problem in the Great Lakes. DNA studies show what’s growing there and why it’s dangerous. Story by .theconversation.com: www.greatlakesnow.org/2025/06/toxi...

#Windsor #Ontario Researchers detect blue-green algae toxin in Lake Erie earlier than ever before But the levels are low, according to the Great Lakes Environmental Research Lab www.cbc.ca/news/canada/...

Toxic Toledo: In our first comics-format story — a collaboration with Crucial Comix — learn why Lake Erie suffers from regular outbreaks of toxic algae that leave residents without access to clean water. Could restoring wetlands help provide a solution? therevelator.org/ohio-toxic-a...
OUR pool has the most algae!!! THANK YOU FOR YOUR ATTENTION TO THE MATTER!!!

In the fight against toxic algae blooms, researchers are giving natural and synthetic clay a shot. The results will help inform scientists about whether the treatment methods could be used on a larger scale, such as Lake Erie, where harmful algal blooms are a costly annual problem.

Investment in this detection and forecast is vital to keeping the drinking water and recreation in Lake Erie safe for Ohioans. After the 2014 Toledo water crisis, we created Daily HABs forecast to keep our citizens informed. If funding is taken away, history could repeat itself.

🦐 Bloody red shrimp feed on plankton and algae, which means they could compete with the small fish and other native organisms already in Lake Superior. But they could also be a new food source for larger fish.

Overloading lakes, rivers, & coastal oceans with phosphorus drives the formation of harmful algal blooms and also results in substantial emissions of greenhouse gases such as methane. Many interventions exist. Go to phosphorusalliance.org to learn more. #Pweek #Pweek2026.

I was expecting to see cyanobacteria. We get warnings for this at times when it's very hot in the Great Lakes and inland lakes that are subject to farm fertilizer runoff. Water, heat, & fertilizer are the keys – and they filled the pool from the runoff tainted Potomac River. Galactic incompetence.

chemolithoautotrophy - likely by ammonia-oxidizing archaea - contributes up to 22.5% of winter net primary production in a waterbody connected to Lake Superior - cool! .bsky.social

Michigan and Ohio are both struggling to reduce the fertilizer runoff getting into Lake Erie which feeds cyanobacterial blooms, also called harmful algal blooms. Those toxic blooms can be hazardous to people and animals.

you don't have to be in the water to be exposed to toxins in cyanobacterial blooms: new work from Greg Boyer's group at the Great Lakes Center for Freshwaters and Human Health www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10....

Each summer, Lake Erie is plagued by toxic cyanobacterial blooms fed by phosphorus runoff from farm fields. Michigan is struggling to reduce the pollution by 40%. Story by .bsky.social: www.greatlakesnow.org/2025/06/new-...

'Every year, Lake Erie experiences seasonal blooms of cyanobacteria, which produce blue-green toxins that pose health risks to humans and animals. …scientists consistently monitored these events—and realized that the situation was actually more dire than they thought.' gizmodo.com/theres-a-dea...

My friend sent me some driftwood from Lake Michigan, I’m using it as the backbone for my shrimp bowl project! I used lava rock for the base to provide more surface area for beneficial bacteria

(2/2) This project is aiming to fill sizeable gaps in our understanding of how Great Lakes nutrient cycles, food webs, and other conditions change during the winter. This is crucial for keeping humans and habitats safe from algal blooms, climate impacts, food web collapse, and other challenges.
